---
title: "Notebook33"
solutions-release-date: "2026-09-04"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
it_city = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/it_cities.csv")
prov = funs.read_file(ub + "data/it_province.geojson")
```

### Questions

This notebook brings back both Italy tables from the last two notebooks and
puts them together. `it_city` is the table of 388 cities, with `city_name`,
`lon`, `lat`, and `pop`; `prov` is the GeoJSON of Italy's 107 provinces, with
a `province` name and a polygon `geometry`. A row of `it_city` is one city; a
row of `prov` is one whole province boundary. The two are not linked by any
shared key column this time: there is no province name on the city table.
What links them is geography: each city sits inside exactly one province, and
the point of a spatial join is to recover that relationship from the
geometries alone.

Spatial operations only work when both tables are in the same coordinate
system, so the first question projects both into EPSG:7791 and saves them
back. After that, the join and geometry-building steps get reassigned; the
plain lookups and counts should just be printed.

1. In the first block, build a point `geometry` for `it_city` with
`it_city.geo.points_from_xy("lon", "lat")`, project it into EPSG:7791 and save
it back, and project `prov` into EPSG:7791 as well and save that too. In the
second block print `it_city`'s `_crs` column; in the third, print `prov`'s, to
confirm they now agree.

```{python}
it_city = it_city.geo.points_from_xy("lon", "lat").geo.to_crs(7791)
prov = prov.geo.to_crs(7791)
```

```{python}
it_city.select(c._crs.first())
```

```{python}
prov.select(c._crs.first())
```

**Answer**: Both tables now report EPSG:7791. That agreement is the
precondition for everything below: a spatial join reads coordinates from both
tables and compares them directly, so if one were still in degrees and the
other in meters the numbers would not line up and the match would be garbage.
Projecting both to the same CRS first is the spatial version of making sure
two key columns hold the same kind of value before a regular join.

2. Join the cities into the provinces with `it_city.geo.sjoin(prov)` and print
the result. Each city point gets matched to the province polygon that contains
it. Look at the columns: what does a row mean now, and what are `index_right`
and the `geometry` column showing?

```{python}
it_city.geo.sjoin(prov)
```

**Answer**: Each row is now a city paired with the province it falls inside,
carrying the city's four original columns plus the `province` name it landed
in. `index_right` records which row of the `prov` table matched, a bookkeeping
column GeoPandas adds. The `geometry` column holds the city *point*, not the
province polygon, because the left table's geometry is the one that survives a
spatial join. So this is still one row per city, now labeled with its
province.

3. A spatial join is an inner join by default, so a city that fell outside
every province polygon would be dropped with no error. Guard against that:
compare the row count of the join to the row count of `it_city`. In the first
block, print the row count of the join; in the second, print the row count of
`it_city`. Did every city match?

```{python}
it_city.geo.sjoin(prov).select(pl.len())
```

```{python}
it_city.select(pl.len())
```

**Answer**: Both come back 388, so no city was dropped: every one of the 388
cities fell inside some province. That is the check worth building a habit
around. A regular join tells you nothing went wrong by matching on a key you
can see; a spatial join matches on geometry you can't eyeball, and a point
sitting just off the coast or in a gap between polygons would simply vanish
from the result without complaint. Counting rows before and after is the
geometry version of the `n_unique() == len()` key check from the last
notebook.

4. Reverse the join with `prov.geo.sjoin(it_city)`, so the province polygons
are kept instead. This gives one row per city again, but now the province
geometry is repeated for each city inside it. Group by `province` and count
the cities per province, then print the five provinces with the most.

```{python}
(
    prov
    .geo.sjoin(it_city)
    .group_by(c.province)
    .agg(n = pl.len())
    .sort(c.n, descending=True)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: Naples leads with 36 cities over 20,000 people, then Milan (23),
Bari (18), Rome (15), and Turin (14). Putting the polygon table first flips
which geometry is kept. Now each row carries a province boundary, duplicated
once per city it contains, the same one-to-many shape a regular left join
produces when the right table has several matches per key. Napoli topping the
list fits the map: the area around Naples is one of the most densely
town-packed corners of the country.

5. Use the join to answer a question neither table can answer alone: how much
of each province's population lives in these cities? Join the cities into the
provinces, group by `province`, sum `pop`, and print the six provinces with
the largest urban population.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .geo.sjoin(prov)
    .group_by(c.province)
    .agg(urban_pop = c.pop.sum())
    .sort(c.urban_pop, descending=True)
    .head(6)
)
```

**Answer**: Rome's province tops the list at about 2.96 million, then Naples
(2.35M), Milan (2.05M), Turin (1.32M), Bari (0.87M), and Palermo (0.81M). This
number could not be read off either table by itself: the population lives on
the city table, the province boundary lives on the polygon table, and only the
spatial join tells us which cities belong to which province. Note the count is
population in *towns over 20,000*, not the full province population, since the
city table left out everything smaller, the same 20,000 floor from
notebook31 that a raw sum quietly inherits.

6. The city table has repeated names. Filter the join to the rows where
`city_name` is `"Massa"` and print `city_name`, `pop`, and `province`. Two
things are worth noticing: which provinces the three Massas land in, and what
their `pop` values are.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .geo.sjoin(prov)
    .filter(c.city_name == "Massa")
    .select(c.city_name, c.pop, c.province)
)
```

**Answer**: Three separate points named Massa land in three different
provinces (Massa-Carrara, Pistoia, and Macerata), and the spatial join sends
each to the right one from its coordinates, which a join on the name alone
could never do. But all three carry the identical population 64,783, which is
the figure for the real Massa in Massa-Carrara. That repetition is a data
error: the population was copied onto every place sharing the name rather than
measured for each. The join is what exposes it, because the same number sitting
next to three different provinces is only obvious once each point is in its
geographic context. It is also a reason to treat the per-province sums from
question 5 as a little high: a few of those towns are carrying a population
that is not really theirs.

7. Provinces can be joined to *themselves*. Use `prov.geo.sjoin(prov,
predicate="touches")` to find every pair of provinces that share a border, and
print how many rows come back.

```{python}
prov.geo.sjoin(prov, predicate="touches").select(pl.len())
```

**Answer**: 476 rows. The `touches` predicate matches two polygons whenever
they share at least one point of their boundaries, so this pairs each province
with every neighbor it shares a line with. The count is even because every
border shows up twice: if Milan touches Pavia then Pavia touches Milan, and
both directed pairs are in the table. There are 238 actual borders here; the
join reports each one from both sides, which is something to remember before
counting anything off a self-join like this.

8. Pull out one province's neighbors. Filter the `touches` self-join to the
rows where `province_left` is `"Milano"` and print the `province_right`
values. Do these match what you would expect from a map of Lombardy?

```{python}
(
    prov
    .geo.sjoin(prov, predicate="touches")
    .filter(c.province_left == "Milano")
    .select(c.province_right)
    .sort(c.province_right)
)
```

**Answer**: Milan borders seven provinces: Bergamo, Cremona, Lodi, Monza e
della Brianza, Novara, Pavia, and Varese. Every one is a real neighbor: Monza
and Lodi were carved out of Milan's own hinterland, Pavia and Cremona sit to
the south, and Novara is just across the boundary into Piedmont. Because the
suffix landed on both name columns in the self-join, the province we filtered
on is `province_left` and its neighbors come back as `province_right`; reading
those apart is the whole trick to using a self-join's output.

9. Not every province has that many neighbors. Count neighbors per province
from the `touches` join (group by `province_left`, count), sort ascending, and
print the five with the fewest. What do the provinces at the bottom have in
common?

```{python}
(
    prov
    .geo.sjoin(prov, predicate="touches")
    .group_by(c.province_left)
    .agg(n = pl.len())
    .sort(c.n)
    .head(5)
)
```

**Answer**: Trieste and Cagliari come in with a single land neighbor each,
then Gorizia, Trapani, and Vibo Valentia with two. The bottom of this list is
geography, not data trouble: Cagliari and Trapani are on islands (Sardinia and
Sicily), so most of their perimeter is coastline that touches no other
province, and Trieste and Gorizia are pinned into the far northeastern corner
against the Slovenian border, where the neighbors that would round out their
counts are in another country and not in this table at all. A province's
border count reflects how much of its edge is shared Italian land versus sea
or an international line.

10. Now switch from overlap to proximity. Use `it_city.geo.sjoin_nearest(
it_city, exclusive=True, distance_col="distance")` to find each city's nearest
other city. Sort by `distance` descending and print the six most isolated
cities with their distance in kilometers (`c.distance / 1000`).

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .geo.sjoin_nearest(it_city, exclusive=True, distance_col="distance")
    .sort(c.distance, descending=True)
    .select(c.city_name_left, c.city_name_right, (c.distance / 1000).round(1).alias("km"))
    .head(6)
)
```

**Answer**: The most isolated pairs are Olbia and Nuoro on Sardinia, about 69
km apart, then Imperia reaching to Cuneo across the Ligurian mountains, and
Oristano to Iglesias back on Sardinia. `exclusive=True` stops each city from
matching itself at distance zero, and because the table is projected the
distances land in meters, so dividing by 1000 gives real kilometers. The
cities that top the list are exactly the ones a map would predict: island
towns and mountain towns, where the next city over 20,000 is genuinely far.

11. The distances in question 10 are only meaningful because the table was
projected. Repeat the nearest-neighbor join on an *unprojected* copy of the
cities and look at what happens to the ranking. In the first block, load the cities fresh and build points but
leave them in EPSG:4326. In the second block, run the same join and print the
four most isolated by `distance`. Does the top of the list still agree with
question 10?

```{python}
it_deg = (
    pl.read_csv(ub + "data/it_cities.csv")
    .geo.points_from_xy("lon", "lat")
)
```

```{python}
(
    it_deg
    .geo.sjoin_nearest(it_deg, exclusive=True, distance_col="distance")
    .sort(c.distance, descending=True)
    .select(c.city_name_left, c.city_name_right, c.distance.round(4).alias("deg"))
    .head(4)
)
```

**Answer**: The ranking changes at the top. In degrees, Imperia and Cuneo come
out as the most isolated pair, ahead of Olbia and Nuoro, which was the clear
leader in kilometers. Nothing raised an error. The join ran fine and returned
distances, just distances in degrees, which are not a fixed length on the
ground. A degree of longitude is shorter than a degree of latitude at these
latitudes, so an east-west gap like Imperia to Cuneo gets undercounted less
than the mostly north-south Sardinian pairs, and the order shuffles. The
projected answer from question 10 is the honest one; this is the same trap as
computing area in unprojected degrees back in notebook32, now showing up as a
ranking that quietly reorders itself.
